items.title: Respect the fit/transform boundary
items.avoid: fit or fit_transform on validation, test, or live traffic.
items.prefer: Fit extractors inside training folds only; persist fitted state for serving.
items.why: Extractor state is part of the model artifact; changing it changes feature column semantics.
items.example: joblib.dump(pipe, "review_clf.joblib") then pipe.predict(["late delivery"])—never refit vec in the API handler.

items.title: Pick vectorizer to match model and corpus size
items.avoid: Default CountVectorizer on million-token logs without max_features or hashing.
items.prefer: CountVectorizer + max_features/min_df for interpretable baselines; HashingVectorizer for streaming; TfidfVectorizer for linear separators.
items.why: Memory, latency, and model assumptions all depend on representation choice.
items.example: 10M unique tokens → HashingVectorizer(n_features=2**20); 5k labeled emails → TfidfVectorizer(max_features=8000).

items.title: Use n-grams deliberately for negation and phrases
items.avoid: Unigram-only vectors when bigrams carry meaning ("not happy", "credit card").
items.prefer: ngram_range=(1,2) or (1,3) with max_features cap to control dimensionality.
items.why: Bag-of-words loses order; n-grams recover short phrases at the cost of wider sparse matrices.
items.example: TfidfVectorizer(ngram_range=(1,2)) creates columns "not" and "not good" separately—better sentiment signal than unigrams alone.

items.title: Inspect features after fit
items.avoid: Training blind when accuracy is poor.
items.prefer: Call get_feature_names_out(), print top tokens per class or highest column sums.
items.why: Mis-tokenization, wrong stop_words, or html artifacts show up immediately in column names.
items.example: Seeing "nbsp", "href", "com" in top features signals you need HTML stripping upstream in cleaning, not more model tuning.
